tip: Sleep under insecticide-treated mosquito nets every night to prevent malaria.
Wash hands with soap and clean water before eating and after using the toilet.
Drink only boiled or treated water to prevent diarrhea and cholera.
Ensure children are vaccinated according to the national immunization schedule.
Eat a balanced diet including fruits and vegetables to boost immunity.
Avoid close contact with people who have symptoms of respiratory infections.
Keep living spaces well ventilated to reduce the risk of airborne infections.
Pregnant women should attend antenatal care visits regularly.
Use oral rehydration salts (ORS) when experiencing dehydration due to diarrhea.
Dispose of waste properly to prevent the spread of infections.
Boil milk and food items if unsure about their safety to prevent foodborne illnesses.
Keep rodents away from food storage to prevent Lassa fever and other rodent-borne diseases.
Cover mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing to prevent spread of infections.
Regularly clean and disinfect frequently touched surfaces at home.
Avoid walking barefoot to prevent soil-transmitted infections.
Check for early signs of malaria (fever, chills, body aches) and seek treatment immediately.
Encourage exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months to boost infant immunity.
Ensure proper spacing between latrines and water sources to prevent contamination.
Use masks in crowded indoor areas during outbreaks of respiratory diseases.
Regularly deworm children to prevent parasitic infections.
Use safe delivery practices and skilled birth attendants for maternal health.
Maintain proper nutrition for children under five to prevent stunting.
Avoid open defecation to prevent spread of cholera and diarrhea.
Clean water containers and storage regularly to prevent mosquito breeding.
Practice safe sex and use condoms to prevent HIV/AIDS and STIs.
Avoid overcrowding in homes to reduce respiratory infections.
Use mosquito repellents in addition to nets for malaria prevention.
Educate communities about the importance of vaccination.
Monitor children for signs of malnutrition and seek early intervention.
Regularly check blood pressure to prevent hypertension-related complications.
Exercise regularly to maintain healthy weight and prevent diabetes.
Use safe and clean cooking practices to prevent respiratory and burns injuries.
Stay hydrated, especially in hot climates, to prevent heat-related illnesses.
Ensure proper handling and storage of medications to prevent misuse.
Avoid drinking untreated surface water to prevent waterborne diseases.
Promote handwashing with soap in schools and public places.
Seek immediate medical care for persistent fever, cough, or diarrhea.
Encourage routine growth monitoring of infants and children.
Use insect repellent clothing and sprays in areas with high mosquito prevalence.
Maintain proper hygiene of food preparation areas.
Practice safe disposal of used syringes and medical waste.
Educate communities on the signs and symptoms of common diseases.
Avoid sharing personal items like toothbrushes or razors.
Keep animals away from food preparation areas to prevent zoonotic diseases.
Ensure proper ventilation in kitchens and cooking areas.
Use fluoride toothpaste and promote oral hygiene in children.
Avoid smoking and exposure to second-hand smoke to prevent respiratory illness.
Regularly clean latrines and bathrooms to prevent infection spread.
Educate about safe use of herbal medicines and avoid unverified remedies.
Encourage community participation in vaccination campaigns.
Store chemicals and cleaning agents safely to prevent accidental poisoning.
Use proper first-aid for cuts and injuries to prevent infection.
Report unusual disease patterns to local health authorities promptly.
